title: Hilt тАФ Dependency Injection
objectives: Understand Hilt/DI via a real-life analogy
Set up Hilt in an Android project
Inject dependencies into Activities, Fragments, ViewModels, Workers

sections.markdown:
**Think of a restaurant kitchen.**

- **Without DI (bad):** the head chef also has to shop for the ingredients, sharpen the knives, milk the cows, and grow the vegetables. Every time she cooks a dish, she does ALL of it. Slow, error-prone, impossible to swap suppliers.
- **With DI (good):** ingredients, knives, cookware тАФ everything is **delivered** to the chef exactly when she needs it, from suppliers she doesn't have to know or manage. She just cooks. If tomorrow you want to test her recipes without a real oven, you can swap in a toy oven тАФ she never notices.
- **Hilt is the delivery system for Android** тАФ a specialized version of Dagger 2 with pre-configured 'delivery routes' for Android lifecycles. You say 'this ViewModel needs a Repository, which needs a Room DAO and a Retrofit API'. Hilt sees the whole graph and delivers each dependency at the right moment, with the right lifecycle scope тАФ Activity-scoped, Singleton, or per-Fragment.

**Why it matters:** without DI, testing is nearly impossible (you can't swap real network calls for fakes), and code gets tangled (constructor calls for real dependencies scattered everywhere). Hilt makes DI approachable for Android teams that were scared off by raw Dagger's steep learning curve.

**Hilt** is Google's opinionated wrapper over **Dagger 2** тАФ a compile-time dependency injection framework. Hilt pre-configures Dagger for Android's specific lifecycles (Application, Activity, Fragment, ViewModel, Service) so you don't have to. It generates all the wiring code at compile time тАФ no reflection, no runtime cost, and errors show up when you build, not when the user opens the app.
## The three annotations you'll use 90% of the time

rows: Marks the Application class тАФ Hilt's entry point.
Marks a class as a DI receiver.
Every Activity, Fragment, Service, BroadcastReceiver you want to inject into.
Tells Hilt how to construct THIS class.
Marks a ViewModel for Hilt-provided dependencies.
Provide dependencies Hilt can't build itself (interfaces, third-party).
One module per concern (network, DB, cache).
A method inside a module that returns the dep.
Tell Hilt 'this interface = this impl'; more efficient than @Provides for that case.

sections.markdown: ## A complete minimal Hilt setup
## Hilt's component scopes (the lifecycles)

**The key insight:** dependencies you `@Provides` in a `SingletonComponent` module are created once per app; those in `ActivityComponent` are created per activity, and so on. Get this wrong and you leak or duplicate тАФ putting an Activity in a Singleton scope is a classic memory leak.
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Forgetting `@HiltAndroidApp` on the Application class.** No entry point тЖТ no injection anywhere. Symptom: `MyViewModelFactory not found` or Hilt-related crashes on first inject.
**Mistake 2: Forgetting `@AndroidEntryPoint` on an Activity/Fragment.** Symptom: crash 'Hilt Fragment must be attached to a @AndroidEntryPoint activity.'
**Mistake 3: Injecting an Activity/Context into a Singleton.** Activity-scoped things must not leak into app-lifetime singletons. Use `@ApplicationContext` for app-lifetime code that needs a Context.
**Mistake 4: Using `@Provides` for a simple interfaceтЖТimpl mapping.** Prefer `@Binds` in an abstract module тАФ the compiler generates a shorter, more efficient factory.

**Mistake 5: Circular dependency.** A needs B, B needs A. Hilt crashes at compile time (great!), but it's still a design smell. Refactor by introducing a lazy provider (`Provider<A>`) or splitting responsibilities.
## Best practices
**1. Prefer constructor injection.** Everything Hilt can build itself (`@Inject constructor(...)`) doesn't need a module. Modules are for interfaces and third-party classes only.
**2. One module per concern.** `NetworkModule`, `DatabaseModule`, `RepositoryModule` тАФ easier to read and swap in tests.
**3. Use `@Binds` for interfaceтЖТimpl.** Cheaper than `@Provides` and communicates the intent.
**4. Scope with intent.** `@Singleton` for truly app-lifetime (Retrofit, Room, OkHttp). Everything else can usually be unscoped (a new instance each time тАФ cheap for stateless objects).
**5. Use `@ApplicationContext` / `@ActivityContext` to disambiguate Contexts.** Hilt provides both; don't guess.
**6. Swap modules in tests with `@TestInstallIn` or `@UninstallModules`.** Replace real Retrofit with a fake in tests without touching production code.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) тАФ Hello Hilt.** Set up `@HiltAndroidApp`, `@AndroidEntryPoint`, inject a `Greeter` class with `@Inject constructor`, display the greeting.

**Why:** Prove the wiring works end-to-end.

**Practice 2 (Medium) тАФ Retrofit + Room via Hilt.** Provide Retrofit + Room via modules; inject Repository into a ViewModel; render data.

**Why:** The standard production stack.

**Practice 3 (Medium) тАФ `@Binds` an interface.** Introduce a `LogService` interface with `LogServiceImpl`; `@Binds` in an abstract module.

**Why:** Feel the difference between @Provides and @Binds.

**Practice 4 (Hard) тАФ Multi-module.** Split into `:app`, `:feature-home`, `:data`. Feature module depends on data; data provides Room DAOs; Hilt wires it all.

**Why:** Real multi-module apps look like this.

**Practice 5 (Expert) тАФ Test replacement.** Use `@TestInstallIn` to swap the real Retrofit with a fake `UserApi` in an instrumented test.

**Why:** DI's biggest payoff is testability.
## Interview Q&A тАФ with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Hilt and how does it relate to Dagger?**

**A:** Hilt is Google's opinionated wrapper over Dagger 2. **Dagger 2** is a general-purpose compile-time DI framework тАФ powerful but with a steep learning curve; you set up components, subcomponents, and scopes manually. **Hilt** pre-configures all of that for Android's specific lifecycles: `SingletonComponent`, `ActivityComponent`, `FragmentComponent`, `ViewModelComponent`, etc. Instead of hand-wiring every component's parent-child relationship, you use annotations like `@HiltAndroidApp`, `@AndroidEntryPoint`, `@HiltViewModel` and Hilt does the rest. All Hilt-generated code is still Dagger under the hood тАФ same compile-time safety, same zero runtime cost. If Dagger is a professional kitchen with every tool laid out, Hilt is that kitchen with an experienced sous-chef who's already prepped everything for Android.

**Q2: Why should you use DI on Android?**

**A:** Four reasons: **(1) Testability** тАФ with DI, you can inject fake Retrofit / Room / clock / repository instances in tests, without touching production code. Without DI, tests must either use real dependencies (slow, flaky) or wire elaborate mocks manually. **(2) Decoupling** тАФ classes depend on interfaces, not concrete constructors; swapping implementations is trivial. **(3) Lifecycle safety** тАФ Hilt gives you Activity-, Fragment-, ViewModel-, and Singleton-scoped instances; the compiler prevents leaks like injecting an Activity into a Singleton. **(4) Boilerplate reduction** тАФ modern Android apps have deep dependency trees (VM тЖТ Repo тЖТ DAO + API + Cache + Analytics); manually wiring `Repository(dao, api, cache, analytics)` everywhere is untenable.

**Q3: `@Provides` vs `@Binds` тАФ when to use which?**

**A:** **`@Provides`** тАФ a method in a module that returns the dependency. Use for anything that requires construction logic: `@Provides fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder().build()`. **`@Binds`** тАФ an abstract method that tells Hilt 'when someone asks for interface I, give them impl class C'. Use for simple interfaceтЖТimplementation mappings when the impl has `@Inject constructor`: `@Binds abstract fun bindLogService(impl: LogServiceImpl): LogService`. **`@Binds` is more efficient** тАФ Dagger generates a shorter factory and skips one delegation. **Rule:** interface-to-impl тЖТ `@Binds`; anything else тЖТ `@Provides`.

**Q4: What are Hilt scopes and why do they matter?**

**A:** Scopes tie a dependency to a specific lifecycle. `@Singleton` тАФ one instance per app (Retrofit, Room, OkHttp). `@ActivityRetainedScoped` тАФ survives config changes, like a ViewModel (great for cross-fragment state). `@ActivityScoped` тАФ one instance per Activity. `@ViewModelScoped` тАФ one per ViewModel. Unscoped тАФ a fresh instance every injection. **Why scopes matter:** (1) memory leaks тАФ if you scope an Activity-context dependency as `@Singleton`, the Activity is kept alive forever. (2) State consistency тАФ a `@Singleton Cache` shares one map across the app; an `@ActivityScoped Cache` gets a fresh map every Activity. **Rule:** default to unscoped; scope only when you specifically need instance sharing.

**Q5: How do you inject a ViewModel with Hilt?**

**A:** Three steps. **(1)** Annotate the ViewModel with `@HiltViewModel` and its constructor with `@Inject`:
```kotlin
@HiltViewModel
class HomeViewModel @Inject constructor(private val repo: UserRepository) : ViewModel()
```
**(2)** Annotate the Activity/Fragment with `@AndroidEntryPoint`. **(3)** Retrieve the VM with `by viewModels()` (Fragment/Activity) or `by activityViewModels()`. Hilt registers its own `ViewModelProvider.Factory` behind the scenes; you never write a factory yourself. To pass runtime arguments (like an ID from navigation), use `SavedStateHandle` тАФ Hilt provides it automatically if your ViewModel's constructor requests it.

**Q6: What's `@AndroidEntryPoint` and where do you use it?**

**A:** `@AndroidEntryPoint` marks a class as a Hilt-injection target: Hilt generates a subclass at build time with all `@Inject` fields populated. Apply it to **every Activity, Fragment, Service, BroadcastReceiver, and View** that has `@Inject`ed dependencies or uses `by viewModels()`. **Rule:** if you use `@Inject lateinit var` or Hilt-provided view models in a class, that class needs `@AndroidEntryPoint`. Without it, injections silently no-op and you get NPEs at runtime. Hilt's error messages are clear about it: 'X must be attached to a @AndroidEntryPoint activity.'

**Q8: What's the runtime cost of Hilt?**

**A:** **Zero at runtime.** All wiring is generated at compile time тАФ Hilt writes Kotlin/Java classes that construct your dependencies using regular `new` calls. No reflection, no runtime scanning. The compile-time cost is real (annotation processing lengthens builds), and this is why the ecosystem is moving to **KSP (Kotlin Symbol Processing)** тАФ Hilt supports it in recent versions, cutting annotation processing time roughly in half compared to the older KAPT. So the trade-off is: slightly slower builds for dramatically less boilerplate, better testability, and safer lifecycle management.

## Real-world usage

- **Every modern production Android app** uses Hilt or Dagger 2.
- **Google's Now In Android** and other reference apps тАФ Hilt throughout.
- **Kotlin Multiplatform** тАФ Hilt is Android-only; on iOS/desktop use Koin, kotlin-inject, or manual DI.
- **Testing patterns** тАФ `@TestInstallIn` for integration tests, plain constructor injection with fakes for unit tests.
- **Multi-module apps** тАФ Hilt encourages a `:core` module for common types and per-feature modules for their own dependencies.
- **Migrating from Dagger 2 to Hilt** тАФ a common task in enterprise codebases; feasible incrementally.

## Advanced notes

- **Qualifiers** тАФ `@Named("api")` or a custom `@Qualifier` annotation lets you inject two impls of the same type (e.g. two different `OkHttpClient`s).
- **Assisted injection** (`@AssistedInject`, `@AssistedFactory`) тАФ pass runtime arguments to constructors Hilt otherwise builds.
- **`Provider<T>`** тАФ inject `Provider<Foo>` for lazy or repeatable construction (avoids circular deps).
- **`Lazy<T>`** тАФ inject `Lazy<Foo>` when you might not need it; `.get()` builds on first access.
- **`@EntryPoint`** тАФ Hilt injection at arbitrary points (e.g. inside Content Providers, Workers before hiltWorker was added).
- **`@HiltWorker`** тАФ inject dependencies into WorkManager `Worker`s.
- **Migrating to KSP** тАФ Hilt 2.44+ supports KSP; switch by replacing `kapt` with `ksp` in Gradle for faster builds.
- **Testing DI graph** тАФ `HiltTestApplication` + `@HiltAndroidTest` for full integration tests.

flashcards.back: Google's opinionated wrapper over Dagger 2 with Android-lifecycle-aware components.

flashcards.front: How to inject into an Activity/Fragment?

flashcards.back: @Provides for logic; @Binds for interfaceтЖТimpl mapping. @Binds is more efficient.
Zero тАФ all wiring generated at compile time.

quiz.q: Hilt is built on top ofтАж

quiz.explain: All code generated at compile time.
Retrofit is built via a Builder, needs @Provides.

challenges.text: Add @HiltAndroidApp, @AndroidEntryPoint, and inject a Greeter into a Fragment.
Provide a Retrofit singleton via a NetworkModule.
Use @Binds to wire a LogService interface to its implementation.
Inject a Repository into a @HiltViewModel and render its data.
Add a @Qualifier to inject two different OkHttpClients (auth vs analytics).
Multi-module setup: :app, :feature-home, :data. Provide Room from :data, inject Repository in :feature-home.
Use @TestInstallIn to replace the real API with a fake in an instrumented test.
